text: Hello, and thank you for taking the time to view our presentation. We will be showing the robotic repair of a traumatic abdominal wall hernia. The authors have nothing to disclose. This is a sixty-three-year-old male with bilateral inguinal hernia pain, left worse than right. He had noticed the bulges about one year prior, but his pain became significantly worse six weeks ago. His medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis and a seven-foot fall from a ladder one year prior. Here's a CT scan after his fall showing a pelvic ring disruption with pubic symphysis diastasis containing the anterior portion of his bladder. On the left, there also appears to be an inguinal hernia more pronounced than the right.

The patient underwent an open reduction and internal fixation of this pelvic ring disruption by orthopedics. He also had a prior umbilical hernia repair. His physical examination was consistent with his history showing bilateral inguinal hernias. The patient was planned for a bilateral robotic assisted inguinal hernia repair. However, intraoperatively he was also found to have a midline incisional and a left abdominal wall disruption.

The surgeon enters the abdomen through the left upper quadrant, placing bilateral eight millimeter ports and a twelve millimeter camera port in the midline. A peritoneal flap is created and extended to the left side, with dissection proceeding laterally to the psoas muscle and inferiorly towards Cooper's ligament. The dissection reveals a traumatic disruption in the left groin and a midline incisional hernia. Chord structures are identified and separated from the peritoneum, completing the dissection of the myopectineal orifice to prepare for mesh placement.

The video shows the dissected space with disrupted anatomy due to the traumatic hernia, occupying the area medial to the lateral umbilical fold. The vas deferens and gonadal vessels are clearly visible. The surgeon completes the flap dissection, extending it towards the right side of the abdomen, reaching the psoas muscles and Cooper's ligament, creating a large peritoneal flap.

The surgeon identifies a small right-sided inguinal hernia and a cord lipoma. The hernia sac is reduced, and the dissection of the flap continues off the cord structures. The preperitoneal flap is fully dissected, revealing the entire left-sided myopectenial orifice and three hernias, including a ventral incisional hernia.

The surgeon closes the lower midline incisional hernia using an O-stratofix suture in a running fashion. The same suture is used to repair the traumatic hernia and left inguinal disruption, taking superficial bites of muscle and fascia to avoid nerve entrapment. This technique is employed to prevent mesh eventration and to reconstruct muscular and fascial coverage for mesh placement.

The video shows a side-by-side comparison of the left inguinal region before and after the closure of hernias. The defects are well re-approximated, allowing for the placement of two elliptically cut meshes with overlap in the midline. The mesh is reinforced in the lower midline and secured with Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a running 2-0 V-lock absorbable suture, extending from the central portion to both sides, effectively covering the entire mesh. The abdomen is then desufflated, and all ports are removed.
